meal_description: A breakfast plate featuring two fried eggs, a bowl of diced avocado, and two slices of toasted artisanal bread.

foods.gut_notes: Eggs are a rich source of high-quality protein, choline, and essential vitamins (e.g., Vitamin D) and minerals (e.g., selenium). While not a direct source of prebiotics or probiotics, their nutritional profile supports overall host health, which indirectly contributes to a balanced gut environment. The gut health impact can be influenced by the type of oil used for frying.
foods.name: Fried eggs

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The soluble and insoluble fibers, including pectin, and polyphenols in avocado serve as fermentable substrates, promoting the growth and metabolic activity of Bifidobacterium species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Avocado's fiber and phytonutrient content supports the growth of beneficial lactic acid bacteria, including Lactobacillus species.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus rhamnosus

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fiber fermentation by these beneficial bacteria leads to the production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), particularly butyrate, crucial for gut barrier integrity.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: An excellent source of monounsaturated fats, vitamins, minerals, and potent anti-inflammatory compounds. Its fiber content, including pectin, effectively feeds beneficial gut bacteria, contributing to microbial diversity and SCFA production. However, this portion size may be medium FODMAP for individuals sensitive to polyols (sorbitol).
foods.name: Diced avocado

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fructans (FOS) and resistant starch present in wheat bread can be fermented by Bifidobacterium species, contributing to SCFA production.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch, formed during the toasting and cooling process, provides a substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria like Faecalibacterium prausnitzii.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Primarily provides carbohydrates for energy. If made from refined flour, it offers limited diverse prebiotic fibers compared to whole grains. The high content of fructans (a type of FOS) makes it a high FODMAP food, potentially causing gastrointestinal discomfort in individuals with IBS or FODMAP sensitivities. While some resistant starch forms upon cooling after toasting, its overall prebiotic contribution is modest compared to other food sources.
foods.name: Toasted artisanal bread

meal_description: A classic Western breakfast plate featuring fried eggs, grilled sausages, bacon, sautéed mushrooms, grilled tomato, and grilled white bread.

foods.gut_notes: Refined grain with low fiber content, offering minimal prebiotic benefit. Can be moderate in fructans, potentially triggering symptoms in sensitive individuals. May contribute to a less diverse gut microbiome due to lack of diverse fibers and promotion of general carbohydrate fermenters rather than selective beneficial species.
foods.name: Grilled White Bread

foods.gut_notes: Excellent source of high-quality protein and essential nutrients like choline and vitamin D. Low in FODMAPs and generally well-tolerated. Has a neutral impact on gut bacteria directly, but provides essential building blocks for overall health, including gut integrity.
foods.name: Fried Eggs

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Diets high in processed meats, saturated fats, and potentially nitrates/nitrites, and low in fiber, may indirectly decrease the abundance and activity of butyrate-producing bacteria like Roseburia.
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

foods.gut_notes: Processed meat often contains additives like onion/garlic powder, making it high in FODMAPs. High in saturated fat and potentially Advanced Glycation End products (AGEs) from grilling, which can promote gut inflammation and negatively impact gut barrier integrity. Lacks beneficial fiber and may contribute to a less favorable gut environment.
foods.name: Grilled Breakfast Sausages

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High intake of saturated fats and nitrates/nitrites, common in processed bacon, can negatively impact gut microbial diversity and potentially inhibit growth of beneficial species like F. prausnitzii over time.
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Very high in saturated fat and sodium, and often cured with nitrates/nitrites. Grilling promotes AGE formation, all contributing to oxidative stress and inflammation in the gut lining. Offers no prebiotic benefits and may foster a less diverse and potentially pro-inflammatory gut microbiome.
foods.name: Grilled Bacon Strips

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Beta-glucans in mushrooms selectively promote the growth of Bifidobacterium species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Beta-glucans can also support the growth and activity of various Lactobacillus species.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

foods.gut_notes: Excellent source of prebiotic beta-glucans that nourish beneficial gut bacteria like Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus. Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds. However, a significant portion like this is high in mannitol (a FODMAP), which can cause digestive discomfort (gas, bloating, pain) in sensitive individuals.
foods.name: Sautéed Mushrooms (Cremini)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Dietary polyphenols and fiber (pectin) in tomatoes can indirectly support mucin-degrading bacteria like Akkermansia, contributing to gut barrier health.
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: An excellent anti-inflammatory food, high in lycopene and other antioxidants; grilling can increase lycopene bioavailability. Provides moderate prebiotic fibers like pectin and some resistant starch. Low FODMAP and generally beneficial for gut health, promoting a healthy gut lining and supporting a balanced microbiota.
foods.name: Grilled Tomato Half

meal_description: Three bottles of Yakult fermented milk drinks: Yakult Balance (blue cap), Yakult Original (red cap), and Yakult Plus (green cap).

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Contains a high concentration of the specific probiotic strain Shirota, contributing directly to its population.
bacteria_fed.name: Lacticaseibacillus paracasei

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The fermented milk matrix and small amounts of fermentable sugars may mildly support other beneficial lactic acid bacteria.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus acidophilus

foods.gut_notes: A probiotic-rich fermented dairy drink providing *Lacticaseibacillus paracasei* strain Shirota, beneficial for gut microbiota balance and immune function. It is generally low in FODMAPs due to its small serving size and fermentation process, and contains less sugar than the original variant. Its primary benefit is probiotic delivery.
foods.name: Yakult Balance

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Contains a high concentration of the specific probiotic strain Shirota, contributing directly to its population.
bacteria_fed.name: Lacticaseibacillus paracasei

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The fermented milk matrix and small amounts of fermentable sugars may mildly support other beneficial lactic acid bacteria.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus acidophilus

foods.gut_notes: A concentrated source of the probiotic *Lacticaseibacillus paracasei* strain Shirota, which supports intestinal health and modulates immune responses. While it contains some sugar, the small serving size and probiotic content make its overall gut impact positive. It is considered low FODMAP in this typical serving.
foods.name: Yakult Original

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Contains a high concentration of the specific probiotic strain Shirota, contributing directly to its population.
bacteria_fed.name: Lacticaseibacillus paracasei

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The added prebiotic fiber (dextrin) selectively ferments, supporting the growth of beneficial Bifidobacterium species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The combination of the fermented milk matrix and prebiotic fiber provides a favorable environment for the growth of various beneficial lactic acid bacteria.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus acidophilus

foods.gut_notes: This variant offers the probiotic benefits of *Lacticaseibacillus paracasei* strain Shirota alongside added prebiotic fiber (dextrin), which further supports the growth of other beneficial gut bacteria like Bifidobacteria. The synergy between probiotics and prebiotics (synbiotic effect) enhances its positive impact on gut health and anti-inflammatory pathways. It has less sugar and is suitable for a low FODMAP diet in this serving size.
foods.name: Yakult Plus

meal_description: A child is holding a single-scoop waffle cone filled with a two-toned frozen dessert, appearing to be mixed berry/candy flavored ice cream or sorbet. The colors are bright blue and pink.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: High sugar content provides readily fermentable carbohydrates, which can fuel the growth of opportunistic bacteria like E. coli.
bacteria_fed.name: Escherichia coli

foods.gut_notes: High in sugar, saturated fat, and potentially artificial additives. The high sugar content can contribute to gut dysbiosis by favoring opportunistic bacteria. Lactose (a disaccharide) is high FODMAP and can cause digestive symptoms like bloating and gas in lactose-intolerant individuals if it reaches the colon where it is fermented by various bacteria. It lacks beneficial fibers and anti-inflammatory compounds.
foods.name: Mixed Flavored Ice Cream

foods.gut_notes: Made from refined wheat flour and sugar, the waffle cone offers minimal nutritional value and fiber. Refined carbohydrates can contribute to blood sugar spikes and provide readily available energy for a range of gut bacteria without selectively supporting beneficial species. The fructans present in wheat flour are classified as medium FODMAP and can cause symptoms in sensitive individuals.
foods.name: Waffle Cone

meal_description: Sabudana khichadi with yogurt - a light Indian preparation of tapioca pearls with yogurt

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Minimal resistant starch in processed tapioca; limited butyrate production
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Low mucin-stimulating compounds; refined carbohydrate may reduce mucus-layer quality
bacteria_harmed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: Highly processed, easily digestible but minimal prebiotic value. May cause rapid glucose spikes. Poor for microbial diversity.
foods.name: Sabudana (tapioca pearls)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Primary probiotic strain in yogurt; directly colonizes and survives transit
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus bulgaricus

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Secondary starter culture in yogurt; survives passage through GI tract
bacteria_fed.name: Streptococcus thermophilus

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Lactose and organic acids in yogurt provide modest bifidogenic effect
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Lactic acid production and competitive exclusion by Lactobacillus species
bacteria_harmed.name: Clostridioides difficile

foods.gut_notes: Excellent source of live probiotics. Supports beneficial lactobacilli colonization. Contains lactose which may affect sensitive individuals. Modest anti-inflammatory compounds from lactobacilli metabolites.
foods.name: Yogurt (plain, unsweetened)

meal_description: Single ripe banana

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch and inulin provide selective substrate for bifidogenic fermentation
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Prebiotic fibres support growth of this beneficial species
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Short-chain fatty acid production from pectin fermentation supports this butyrate-producer
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Ripe banana is an excellent prebiotic fruit with minimal FODMAP content suitable for sensitive guts. Pectin promotes mucus layer integrity. Resistant starch content supports commensal bacteria diversity. Anti-inflammatory polyphenols present.
foods.name: Banana (ripe)

meal_description: Milk coffee with biscuits

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Lactose in milk provides minimal fermentation substrate
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Heat treatment reduces any beneficial bacterial viability
bacteria_harmed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

foods.gut_notes: Low prebiotic value. Contains lactose which may cause issues in lactose-sensitive individuals. No probiotics present due to heating.
foods.name: Milk (in coffee)

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Polyphenols and caffeine have antimicrobial properties against pathogenic species
bacteria_harmed.name: Clostridium difficile

foods.gut_notes: Contains polyphenols with mild anti-inflammatory properties. May suppress pathogenic bacteria but minimal prebiotic benefit. Caffeine can increase gut motility.
foods.name: Coffee

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Refined grains provide minimal substrate; negligible impact
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Refined carbohydrates lack resistant starch and fibre needed by butyrate producers
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia faecis

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Low polysaccharide content provides insufficient mucus layer substrate
bacteria_harmed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: Refined wheat biscuits lack fibre and prebiotics. Ultra-processed nature may promote dysbiosis. No resistant starch or complex carbohydrates. Potential contributor to reduced microbial diversity with regular consumption.
foods.name: Biscuits (wheat-based, commercial)

meal_description: Haldiram sev with coffee

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High salt and oxidative stress from frying reduce butyrate-producing capacity
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Inflammatory lipid peroxides from deep frying inhibit short-chain fatty acid production
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia faecis

foods.gut_notes: Refined chickpea flour lacks intact fiber. Deep frying creates oxidative stress and lipopolysaccharide endotoxemia risk. High sodium disrupts osmotic balance and mucus layer.
foods.name: Haldiram sev (fried chickpea noodles)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid) show modest prebiotic potential
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Caffeine may increase intestinal permeability and dysbiosis in sensitive individuals
bacteria_harmed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

foods.gut_notes: Coffee contains polyphenols with modest anti-inflammatory benefits but caffeine can increase tight junction permeability. Mixed impact depending on individual tolerance.
foods.name: Coffee (black, brewed)

meal_description: Biscuits with coffee

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Refined carbohydrates rapidly fermented, may promote pathogenic gram-negative bacteria overgrowth
bacteria_fed.name: Escherichia coli

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Simple sugars and lack of resistant starch favour proteolytic species
bacteria_fed.name: Clostridium perfringens

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Lacks butyrate-generating substrates needed by this beneficial species
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Refined grains lack resistant starch required for short-chain fatty acid production
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia species

foods.gut_notes: Refined biscuits lack fibre and prebiotic compounds. Rapid glycemic spike may promote dysbiosis and reduce beneficial Faecalibacterium prausnitzii abundance.
foods.name: Biscuits (refined grain)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols in coffee may modestly promote growth of this species
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

foods.gut_notes: Coffee provides polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties and may have modest prebiotic effects via polyphenol metabolism. However, caffeine may increase intestinal permeability in sensitive individuals.
foods.name: Coffee

meal_description: Fried pomfret 2-3 cutlets with lettuce

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) provide weak substrate; frying reduces beneficial effect
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High-temperature frying produces oxidative compounds and advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that damage gut barrier
bacteria_harmed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Oxidative stress from fried preparation inhibits butyrate-producing capacity
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia faecis

foods.gut_notes: Pomfret contains beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA ~200-300mg/100g), but deep frying significantly reduces anti-inflammatory benefits and generates harmful compounds. Creates pro-inflammatory environment despite fish's inherent benefits.
foods.name: Fried pomfret cutlets

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Minimal dietary fiber; small amounts of pectin and cellulose provide weak substrate
bacteria_fed.name: Bacteroides vulgatus

foods.gut_notes: Lettuce is very low in fermentable fiber and provides minimal prebiotic benefit. Primary value is water content and phytonutrients. Portion too small (30g) to meaningfully impact microbiota. Contains minimal polyphenols due to pale variety assumption.
foods.name: Lettuce

meal_description: A packet of Peckish Sweet Chilli Flavoured Rice Crackers, held by a person in a kitchen environment where food preparation appears to be ongoing in the background. The primary focus is on the packaged snack.

foods.gut_notes: These are highly processed, air-baked rice crackers made primarily from refined rice. They are very low in dietary fiber and offer minimal prebiotic benefits, though trace amounts of resistant starch (RS3) may be present from the rice. The 'Sweet Chilli' flavouring often contains 'natural flavours' which may include moderate amounts of onion or garlic components, making it a 'medium' FODMAP item for some sensitive individuals, depending on the specific ingredients and amount consumed. It provides very few anti-inflammatory compounds and lacks probiotics. While gluten-free, its refined nature means it contributes little to beneficial gut flora and should be consumed in moderation as part of a balanced diet rich in whole foods.
foods.name: Peckish Sweet Chilli Flavoured Rice Crackers

meal_description: One raw banana with oats, milk, and honey

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch and inulin selectively stimulate growth
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin and resistant starch serve as substrate for butyrate production
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin provides substrate for mucus layer support
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: Excellent prebiotic source with resistant starch content increasing as banana ripeness decreases. Raw preparation preserves all prebiotic compounds.
foods.name: Raw banana

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Beta-glucan is primary substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Beta-glucan fermentation produces short-chain fatty acids
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia faecis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch and soluble fibre promote growth
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

foods.gut_notes: Beta-glucans provide strong prebiotic effect and reduce inflammation through SCFA production. Well-tolerated with low FODMAP profile.
foods.name: Oats

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Lactose may be fermented by some strains if lactase-persistent
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Casein and A1 beta-casein may trigger mild inflammatory response in sensitive individuals
bacteria_harmed.name: Lactobacillus species

foods.gut_notes: Minimal prebiotic benefit unless individual is lactase-persistent. May be inflammatory in lactose-intolerant or A1 casein-sensitive individuals. Consider lactose-free or A2 milk alternatives for optimal gut health.
foods.name: Milk (cow's)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fructose and oligosaccharides in honey selectively feed bifidobacteria
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

foods.gut_notes: Despite high FODMAP classification, small portion (15g) minimizes impact. Excellent anti-inflammatory compounds (polyphenols, enzymes) support gut barrier integrity. Raw honey preferred for retained antimicrobial compounds.
foods.name: Honey

meal_description: A simple breakfast plate consisting of a fried egg, a small metal bowl of diced avocado, and two slices of lightly toasted artisan white bread, likely with black sesame seeds.

foods.gut_notes: A high-quality source of complete protein and essential micronutrients such as choline, selenium, and vitamins A, D, and E. While eggs themselves provide minimal direct dietary fiber or prebiotics, they contribute significantly to satiety and overall nutritional status, indirectly supporting a healthy physiological environment.
foods.name: Fried Egg

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The soluble and insoluble dietary fibers in avocado, along with its polyphenols, are fermented by gut bacteria to produce short-chain fatty acids like butyrate, a key nutrient for this beneficial species.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols and healthy fats found in avocado have been shown to positively influence the growth and activity of this important mucin-degrading bacterium, which contributes to gut barrier integrity.
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Dietary fiber acts as a substrate, promoting the proliferation and metabolic activity of various Lactobacillus species, including L. plantarum.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The fermentable carbohydrates and fiber in avocado support the growth and metabolic functions of Bifidobacterium species, contributing to a healthy gut environment.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

foods.gut_notes: An excellent source of monounsaturated fats, dietary fiber, and a wide array of anti-inflammatory compounds (e.g., carotenoids, tocopherols, various polyphenols). These components nourish beneficial gut bacteria and contribute to overall gut barrier function. However, the estimated portion (80g) is high in sorbitol, a polyol FODMAP, which can trigger digestive symptoms in individuals with IBS or FODMAP sensitivities.
foods.name: Diced Avocado

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch, formed during the toasting and cooling process, along with residual fructans (though a FODMAP), can be fermented by Bifidobacterium species like B. adolescentis.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The fermentation of resistant starch by the gut microbiota leads to the production of butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid beneficial for butyrate-producing species like Roseburia intestinalis.
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

foods.gut_notes: Primarily provides carbohydrates for energy. Toasting can generate some resistant starch, which functions as a prebiotic. While artisan white bread may be considered higher quality than standard white bread, it still likely contains fructans, making it a high FODMAP food. Its refined nature means it offers less diverse fiber and polyphenols compared to whole-grain options. The visible black specks, if nigella or black sesame seeds, would contribute minor additional anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefits.
foods.name: Toasted Artisan White Bread

meal_description: 10gm yogurt and 20gm nuts

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Exogenous probiotic strain commonly present in yogurt cultures
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus delbrueckii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Primary yogurt starter culture strain
bacteria_fed.name: Streptococcus thermophilus

foods.gut_notes: Small portion limits probiotic benefit. Contains lactose but 10gm is negligible. Provides bioavailable calcium and protein.
foods.name: Yogurt (plain, unsweetened assumed)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fiber and polyphenols support butyrate-producing capacity
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Dietary fiber fermentation substrate
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia spp.

foods.gut_notes: 20gm portion is small but provides polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties. Modest prebiotic fiber content. Rich in vitamin E and magnesium which support barrier function.
foods.name: Mixed nuts (composition unspecified)

meal_description: A traditional Indian meal featuring two deep-fried puris (flatbreads) served with chole (chickpea curry). The meal is garnished with sliced red onion, a green chilli, and a lime wedge.

foods.gut_notes: Made from refined wheat flour and deep-fried, puris are high in refined carbohydrates and unhealthy fats. They offer minimal prebiotic fibers, can contribute to gut inflammation due to processing and frying, and may not selectively feed beneficial gut bacteria. High FODMAP (fructans) from wheat can trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals. Overconsumption can negatively impact gut barrier function and microbiome balance.
foods.name: Puri (Deep-fried wheat flatbread)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Rich in GOS (galactooligosaccharides) and resistant starch from chickpeas, which are selectively fermented by this species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Utilizes GOS and resistant starch found abundantly in chickpeas.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch and other complex carbohydrates in chickpeas serve as excellent fuel for this important butyrate-producer.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Ferments resistant starch and dietary fibers from chickpeas to produce short-chain fatty acids, including butyrate.
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

foods.gut_notes: Chole is rich in chickpeas, an excellent source of prebiotic fibers like resistant starch, GOS, and pectin, which strongly nourish beneficial gut bacteria and support butyrate production. The spices commonly used (e.g., turmeric, cumin, ginger) provide powerful anti-inflammatory and antioxidant compounds. However, the high FODMAP content (GOS) can cause significant digestive discomfort in individuals with IBS or FODMAP sensitivities.
foods.name: Chole (Chickpea curry)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Contains fructans (FOS and inulin) that are selectively utilized by Bifidobacterium species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Feeds on the fructans present in red onion, promoting its growth and activity.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

foods.gut_notes: Raw red onion is a potent prebiotic due to its high content of fructans (FOS and inulin), which are excellent fuel for beneficial Bifidobacterium species. It also contains anti-inflammatory compounds like quercetin and anthocyanins. However, its high FODMAP content means it can cause gas and bloating for individuals with FODMAP intolerance.
foods.name: Red Onion (sliced)

foods.gut_notes: Green chillies provide capsaicin, which has some anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. In small amounts, it is low FODMAP. However, high intake can irritate the gut lining in sensitive individuals. It does not significantly contribute to prebiotic fiber intake or directly feed specific beneficial gut bacteria.
foods.name: Green Chilli

foods.gut_notes: Lime provides Vitamin C and flavonoids, which are antioxidants and contribute to anti-inflammatory effects. Its acidity can aid digestion. It is low FODMAP and generally well-tolerated, but does not offer significant prebiotic fiber for gut bacteria.
foods.name: Lime wedge

meal_description: A plate featuring seekh kebabs, served with a side of shredded iceberg lettuce and raw sliced red onion.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Spices like cumin and coriander present in kebabs may offer modest prebiotic effects supporting this species.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Small amounts of onion/garlic powder in seasoning can provide trace fructans, offering minimal support.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

foods.gut_notes: A good source of protein. The spices commonly used (ginger, turmeric, cumin) contribute anti-inflammatory properties. However, if high in saturated fat or cooked at very high temperatures, it could potentially contribute to inflammatory pathways, although this effect is mitigated by beneficial spices. Medium FODMAP due to typical onion and garlic content.
foods.name: Seekh Kebab

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Provides general dietary fiber (cellulose, hemicellulose) which supports a diverse gut microbiome, including Bifidobacterium species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fermentation of non-digestible carbohydrates contributes to butyrate production by key short-chain fatty acid producers.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: A source of hydration and low-FODMAP dietary fiber, which supports general gut motility and feeds beneficial gut bacteria, contributing to overall diversity. Its anti-inflammatory impact is moderate due to its water content and some antioxidants.
foods.name: Shredded Iceberg Lettuce

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Rich in FOS and inulin, which are selectively fermented by Bifidobacterium species, promoting their growth and activity.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: High content of fructans provides a primary substrate for this beneficial species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Certain Lactobacillus species can also ferment FOS, contributing to a healthy gut environment.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus acidophilus

bacteria_fed.mechanism: The fermentation of FOS by other bacteria leads to the production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) like butyrate, which is utilized by F. prausnitzii, a key butyrate producer and anti-inflammatory species.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: An excellent source of potent prebiotics (FOS, inulin) that strongly feed beneficial gut bacteria, and rich in anti-inflammatory flavonoids (quercetin, anthocyanins). However, its high FODMAP content can trigger significant digestive symptoms like gas, bloating, and abdominal pain in individuals with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).
foods.name: Raw Red Onion

meal_description: Rice with spicy prawn curry, cucumber, and spinach paratha

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Refined grains lack resistant starch; may reduce butyrate-producing bacteria
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Refined white rice devoid of bran and germ; minimal prebiotic value. Lacks the resistant starch needed to feed beneficial bacteria. Does not cause dysbiosis but offers no gut health advantage.
foods.name: White rice

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Animal protein source supports proteolytic bacteria
bacteria_fed.name: Bacteroides fragilis

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Excessive animal protein without fibre shifts microbiota balance; may reduce butyrate producers
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High protein, low fibre meal reduces substrates for this SCFA producer
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia faecis

foods.gut_notes: Curry base likely contains onion, garlic (high FODMAP triggers). Spices (chilli, turmeric) may cause inflammation in sensitive individuals. Curcumin in turmeric is anti-inflammatory but offset by high FODMAP load. Prawn provides protein but shifts balance toward proteolytic bacteria.
foods.name: Prawn curry (with spices)

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Mild fibre content supports this versatile lactic acid bacterium
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

foods.gut_notes: Low FODMAP, good for sensitive guts. Contains quercetin (anti-inflammatory polyphenol). Minimal prebiotic impact due to small portion and low fibre density. Hydrating and gentle on gut.
foods.name: Cucumber

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Spinach contains inulin-type fibres; modest amounts feed bifidobacteria
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols in spinach support mucus-layer bacteria
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: Spinach provides iron, lutein, and polyphenols with anti-inflammatory effects. Paratha made with refined wheat flour reduces prebiotic value compared to whole wheat. Fried preparation adds oil which may slow digestion. Medium FODMAP due to small spinach content within larger bread portion.
foods.name: Spinach paratha (half piece)

meal_description: Light breakfast consisting of porridge with milk, roasted gram flour with milk, digestive biscuit, and half banana

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Beta-glucan is fermented to short-chain fatty acids which feed this butyrate-producing species
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Oat beta-glucan supports SCFA production
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia faecis

foods.gut_notes: Small portion limits impact. Beta-glucan provides moderate prebiotic benefit and supports butyrate producers.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Lactose fermentation if lactase-insufficient
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

foods.gut_notes: Minimal prebiotic value unless individual has lactase deficiency. Lactose may cause issues in sensitive individuals.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Inulin and resistant starch content selectively promotes this species
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch fermentation produces butyrate

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Supports SCFA-producing bacteria through carbohydrate fermentation

bacteria_fed.name: Bacteroides vulgatus

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Refined carbohydrates lack the fibre this species requires
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Refined carbohydrate source with minimal fibre and prebiotic value. May feed less beneficial bacteria. Contributes empty calories nutritionally.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin and fructo-oligosaccharides support bifidogenic growth
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin fermentation

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin fermentation produces short-chain fatty acids
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Good prebiotic source with soluble fibre (pectin). Contains polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties. Supports beneficial bacteria growth.
foods.name: Banana

meal_description: Milk coffee with a small slice of lemon cake topped with crème

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Lactose can be weakly fermented to produce short-chain fatty acids
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

foods.gut_notes: Lactose has minor prebiotic properties but milk lacks significant fibre. May cause bloating in lactose-sensitive individuals.

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Polyphenols in coffee may increase gut permeability in susceptible individuals; high caffeine can increase intestinal motility
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Coffee contains polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties but caffeine may increase intestinal permeability and motility. Moderate consumption acceptable for most.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Simple sugars from refined flour and added sugar promote growth of pathogenic Bacteroides species
bacteria_fed.name: Bacteroides vulgatus

bacteria_fed.mechanism: High sugar content creates dysbiotic conditions favouring pathogenic clostridia
bacteria_fed.name: Clostridium difficile

bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High sugar intake associated with reduced mucus-layer colonisation by this protective species
bacteria_harmed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

foods.gut_notes: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content drives dysbiosis. Lemon zest may provide minor polyphenols but insufficient to offset negative impact. High FODMAP due to potential fructose content.

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High saturated fat reduces diversity and abundance of butyrate-producing bacteria
bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: High saturated fat content may reduce beneficial bacteria diversity and increase bile acid dysmetabolism. No prebiotic value.

meal_description: Roasted gram (sattu) with milk, sugar, and salt

bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Minor prebiotic effect from plant polysaccharides
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Lactose can be fermented by some Bifidobacterium species if lactase-persistent
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Unfermented milk provides limited substrate; pasteurization reduces beneficial bacteria
bacteria_harmed.name: Lactobacillus delbrueckii

foods.gut_notes: Impact depends on lactase persistence. Pasteurized milk lacks probiotics. May cause bloating in lactose-intolerant individuals. Contains some anti-inflammatory compounds (lactoferrin, immunoglobulins).

bacteria_harmed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Salt-sensitive species affected by elevated sodium intake
bacteria_harmed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

foods.gut_notes: Minimal amount used is unlikely to cause significant harm, but excess dietary salt is associated with dysbiosis and intestinal barrier dysfunction.

meal_description: Moong dal khichadi with mango pickle and pickled onion slice

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch from cooked moong dal serves as primary substrate
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Prebiotic fibre supports bifidobacterial growth
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

foods.gut_notes: Excellent prebiotic food. Low FODMAP makes it suitable for IBS. Provides resistant starch which increases butyrate production, beneficial for colonocyte health and anti-inflammatory effects.

bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Raw onion contains inulin and FOS which are prebiotic; fermentation provides probiotics

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Lactic acid and antimicrobial compounds from fermentation
bacteria_harmed.name: Clostridium difficile

foods.gut_notes: High FODMAP from onion (fructans). Contains both prebiotics (inulin/FOS) and probiotics from fermentation. May cause symptoms in FODMAP-sensitive individuals despite prebiotic benefit. Quercetin provides anti-inflammatory benefits.

meal_description: Breakfast or brunch featuring two slices of whole-grain toast (one with mashed avocado, one with butter/spread) and a hard-boiled egg seasoned with black pepper.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Rich in fructans (a type of FOS) and resistant starch which are fermented by this species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Utilizes fructans and resistant starch as a primary energy source.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Ferments fructans present in wheat to produce short-chain fatty acids.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: A key degrader of resistant starch, producing beneficial short-chain fatty acids.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Ferments various fibers, including resistant starch, to produce butyrate, vital for gut health.
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Contributes to butyrate production by fermenting dietary fibers from avocado.
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium animalis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Benefits from diverse plant fibers found in avocado, contributing to gut diversity.
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

foods.gut_notes: An excellent source of monounsaturated fats, fiber, and polyphenols, promoting gut diversity and reducing inflammation. Its medium FODMAP (sorbitol) content may cause mild symptoms in sensitive individuals.
foods.name: Mashed avocado

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Rich in fructans (a type of FOS) and resistant starch which are fermented by this species.
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus acidophilus

bacteria_fed.mechanism: A key degrader of resistant starch, producing beneficial short-chain fatty acids.
bacteria_fed.name: Ruminococcus bromii

foods.gut_notes: Similar to the other slice, this bread provides fermentable fibers for beneficial gut bacteria, contributing to microbial diversity and SCFA production. However, its high fructan content can be a concern for sensitive individuals.

foods.gut_notes: Primarily a source of fats and fat-soluble vitamins. While it doesn't directly feed gut bacteria, its butyrate content (especially in grass-fed butter) is crucial for nourishing colonocytes and maintaining gut barrier integrity.
foods.name: Butter

foods.gut_notes: An excellent source of high-quality protein, essential amino acids, and vital micronutrients (e.g., choline, Vitamin D, B12). While it doesn't directly feed gut microbes with fiber, its nutritional profile indirectly supports overall gut health and cellular function, contributing to gut repair and maintenance.
foods.name: Hard-boiled chicken egg

foods.gut_notes: Contains piperine, a bioactive compound with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. These properties can indirectly benefit gut health by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the digestive tract, potentially enhancing nutrient absorption.
foods.name: Black pepper

meal_description: A light snack consisting of a handful of green sprouts, one orange, and a slice of apple

bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia faecis

foods.gut_notes: Excellent prebiotic source. Raw form preserves enzyme activity and heat-sensitive polyphenols with anti-inflammatory effects. Supports beneficial anaerobes.

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin fermentation provides selective growth substrate
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium adolescentis

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Polyphenols support mucus-layer integrity and growth
bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin fermentation generates butyrate precursors
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.name: Bacteroides vulgatus

foods.gut_notes: Excellent source of quercetin and other polyphenols with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Pectin promotes beneficial bacterial diversity. Skin-on consumption (if applicable) enhances prebiotic value.

meal_description: One raw banana 120 gm

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Pectin and inulin selectively feed this beneficial species, promoting butyrate production
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Resistant starch and pectin provide substrate for fermentation

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Prebiotic fibres promote growth of this butyrate-producer
bacteria_fed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: Raw banana provides excellent prebiotic fibre profile with pectin, resistant starch, and trace inulin. Low FODMAP content ensures good tolerability. Rich in polyphenols and magnesium with potent anti-inflammatory effects. Promotes mucus-layer health and strengthens intestinal barrier. Ripeness level affects resistant starch content—greener bananas have higher resistant starch.
foods.name: Banana (raw, unripe to medium ripeness)

bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia intestinalis

meal_description: A single whole egg.

foods.gut_notes: Eggs are a low FODMAP, highly nutritious food providing high-quality protein, choline, selenium, and vitamins A, D, and E. These nutrients are essential for overall health, which indirectly supports a healthy gut environment. Choline, in particular, is vital for liver function and bile production, which aids in fat digestion. While eggs do not contain prebiotic fibers to directly feed specific gut bacteria, their nutrient profile contributes to cellular health and reduces inflammation, fostering an environment where beneficial gut bacteria can thrive. They do not contain compounds known to harm gut bacteria.
foods.name: Whole Egg

meal_description: Kimchi drink with sugar, fried steamed rice, and spinach paratha

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Primary probiotic in kimchi fermentation; survives passage to colon
bacteria_fed.name: Lactobacillus plantarum

bacteria_fed.name: Leuconostoc mesenteroides

bacteria_harmed.name: Clostridium difficile

bacteria_fed.name: Fusobacterium nucleatum

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Sugar-rich environment inhibits butyrate-producing species

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Minimal prebiotic potential; white rice has low resistant starch when fried
bacteria_fed.name: Bacteroides vulgatus

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: High heat cooking destroys resistant starch; promotes gram-negative dysbiosis
bacteria_harmed.name: Roseburia faecis

bacteria_harmed.name: Faecalibacterium prausnitzii

foods.gut_notes: White rice offers minimal gut benefits. Frying increases oxidative stress and advanced glycation end products (AGEs), promoting inflammation. Destroys heat-sensitive resistant starch. Potential dysbiotic shift from repeated reheating and frying.
foods.name: Fried steamed rice

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Spinach inulin and fructans selectively feed Bifidobacterium species
bacteria_fed.name: Bifidobacterium longum

bacteria_fed.mechanism: Fiber substrate for butyrate production; anti-inflammatory pathway
bacteria_fed.name: Roseburia faecis

bacteria_fed.name: Akkermansia muciniphila

bacteria_harmed.mechanism: Spinach polyphenols have mild antimicrobial properties
bacteria_harmed.name: Escherichia coli

foods.gut_notes: Spinach provides excellent prebiotic fiber, polyphenols, and anti-inflammatory compounds (lutein, kaempferol). However, high FODMAP content (fructans, GOS) may trigger bloating/IBS symptoms. Frying significantly reduces polyphenol bioavailability and adds pro-inflammatory oxidized lipids. Contains oxalates which may bind minerals.
foods.name: Spinach paratha (Indian flatbread with spinach)
